q:

(a) UV rays (i) Diagnostic tool in medicine
(b) X-rays (ii) Water purification
(c) Microwave (iii) Communication, Radar
(d) Infrared wave (iv) Improving visibility in foggy days

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:

  List-I   List-II
(a) UV rays (i) Diagnostic tool in medicine
(b) X-rays (ii) Water purification
(c) Microwave (iii) Communication, Radar
(d) Infrared wave (iv) Improving visibility in foggy days

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
solution:
(a) uv rays - used for water purification
(b) x-rays have high penetration power. Therefore, they are used for diagnosing fracture
(c) Microwaves are used for mobile and radar communication
(d) Infrared waves show less scattering therefore used in foggy days

a-ii, b-i, c-iii, d-iv

subject: Physics
q: All electromagnetic wave is transporting energy in the negative <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>z</mi></math> direction. At a certain point and certain time the direction of electric field of the wave is along positive <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi></math> direction. What will be the direction of the magnetic field of the wave at that point and instant?
question: All electromagnetic wave is transporting energy in the negative <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>z</mi></math> direction. At a certain point and certain time the direction of electric field of the wave is along positive <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi></math> direction. What will be the direction of the magnetic field of the wave at that point and instant?
solution:
In an electromagnetic wave, the direction of energy flow is along the direction of the wave propagation. Therefore, the wave propagates along negative z direction. 

Electric field is along the positive y direction and the wave propagates along negative z direction. Thus, magnetic field is in direction perpendicular to both z and y axes. 

Using vector algebra, 

(-k^)=(+j^)×i^

Hence, magnetic field is in positive x direction.

subject: Physics
q: An electromagnetic wave in vacuum has the electric and magnetic fields $\vec{E}$ and $\vec{B}$, which are always perpendicular to each other. The direction of polarization is given by $\vec{X}$ and that of wave propagation by $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{k}}$. Then :
question: An electromagnetic wave in vacuum has the electric and magnetic fields $\vec{E}$ and $\vec{B}$, which are always perpendicular to each other. The direction of polarization is given by $\vec{X}$ and that of wave propagation by $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{k}}$. Then :
solution:
Direction of polarization is parallel to magnetic field,
$\therefore \quad \overrightarrow{\mathrm{X}} \| \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}$
and direction of wave propagation is parallel to $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}$
$\therefore \quad \overrightarrow{\mathrm{K}} \| \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}$

subject: Physics
q: A displacement current of $4.0$ A can be set up in the space between two parallel plates of $6$ $\mu$F capacitor. The rate of change of potential difference across the plates of the capacitor is nearly $\alpha \times 10^6$ V/s. The value of $\alpha$ is __________.
question: A displacement current of $4.0$ A can be set up in the space between two parallel plates of $6$ $\mu$F capacitor. The rate of change of potential difference across the plates of the capacitor is nearly $\alpha \times 10^6$ V/s. The value of $\alpha$ is __________.
solution:
The displacement current $I_d$ between the plates of a capacitor is given by the formula:

$I_d = C \dfrac{dV}{dt}$

Substituting the given values of displacement current and capacitance:

$4.0 = 6 \times 10^{-6} \times \dfrac{dV}{dt}$

$\dfrac{dV}{dt} = \dfrac{4.0}{6 \times 10^{-6}}$

$\dfrac{dV}{dt} = \dfrac{2}{3} \times 10^6 \approx 0.67 \times 10^6$ V/s

Comparing this with the given rate of change of potential difference $\alpha \times 10^6$ V/s, we get $\alpha = 0.67$.

Answer: $0.67$

subject: Physics
q: The frequency of $X$-rays; $\gamma$-rays and ultraviolet rays are respectively $a, b$ and $c$ then
question: The frequency of $X$-rays; $\gamma$-rays and ultraviolet rays are respectively $a, b$ and $c$ then
solution:
Frequency range of $\gamma$-ray, $b=10^{18}-10^{23} \mathrm{~Hz}$
Frequency range of X-ray, $a=10^{16}-10^{20} \mathrm{~Hz}$
Frequency range of ultraviolet ray, $c=10^{15}-10^{17} \mathrm{~Hz}$
$\therefore a < b ; b>c$

question:

B→=B0sinkx+ωtj^ T. Expression for corresponding electric field will be:

(Where c is speed of light)
solution:
For electromagnetic wave speed of light is given by

c=E0B0
Electric field is expressed as E0=cB0 , E→ is ⊥ to direction x and direction of B→

The expression of electric field is E→=cB0 sin⁡(kx+wt)k^  V m-1

subject: Physics
q: The waves emitted when a metal target is bombarded with high energy electrons are
question: The waves emitted when a metal target is bombarded with high energy electrons are
solution: The bombarding electrons can eject electrons from the inner shells of the atoms of the metal target. Those vacancies will be quickly filled by electrons dropping from higher levels, emitting x-rays with sharply defined frequencies associated with the difference between the atomic energy levels of the target atoms. Hence, X-rays are emitted when a metal target is bombarded with high energy electrons.

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following Maxwell’s equation is valid for time varying conditions but not valid for static conditions:
question: Which of the following Maxwell’s equation is valid for time varying conditions but not valid for static conditions:
solution:
Maxwell’s four equations describe the electric and magnetic fields arising from distributions of electric charges and currents, and how those fields change in time.

The equations are as follows:

∮E→·dA→=qε0   ...1∮B⇀·dA⇀=0   ...2∮E⇀·dl⇀=-∂ϕB∂t   ...3∮B⇀·dl⇀=μ0I   ...4

From the above set of equations, it is clear that only the third equation is valid for time varying field.

Hence, this is the correct option.

The transmitted wave will be
3.1cos1.8z-5.4×106ti^ N/C.

The reflected wave will be
3.1cos1.8z+5.4×106ti^ N C-1.
solution: Reflected wave will have direction opposite to incident wave.

The total average energy density of the electromagnetic field of the wave is : [Use <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>ε</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>85</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>12</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>]
question: In a plane EM wave, the electric field oscillates sinusoidally at a frequency of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>10</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math> and an amplitude of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The total average energy density of the electromagnetic field of the wave is : [Use <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>ε</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>85</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>12</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>]
solution:
Total average energy density of the electromagnetic field is given by, UE=12ε0E02.

⇒UE=12×8.85×10-12×502

=1.106×10–8 J m–3

q: An electromagnetic wave of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>GHz</mi></math> enters a dielectric medium of relative electric permittivity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>25</mn></math> from vacuum. The wavelength of this wave in that medium will be _________ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
question: An electromagnetic wave of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>GHz</mi></math> enters a dielectric medium of relative electric permittivity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>25</mn></math> from vacuum. The wavelength of this wave in that medium will be _________ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
λ in vacuum =cf=3×1083×109=0.1 m

∴  λ in medium =0.1μ

Where refractive index

μ=μrεr

Assuming non-magnetic material μr=1

∴  μ=2.25=1.5

λm=0.11.5=115 m=6.67 cm

=667×10-2 cm

question: A plane electromagnetic wave of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MHz</mi></math> propagates in free space along <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-direction. At a particular space and time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>E</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mover><mrow><mi>j</mi><mo> </mo></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. What is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>B</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> at this point?
solution:
It is given that 

 E→=6.6j^( V m-1).

The relation between the electric, magnetic field and speed of light is E→×B→=c→.

Clearly,   B→=B0(k^)

Using the relation,
     cB0=E
⇒B0=Ec=6.63×108=2.2×10-8 T

subject: Physics
q:
Match List I and List II

  List I   List II
A Microwaves I Physiotherapy
B UV rays II Treatment of cancer
C Infra-red rays III Lasik eye surgery
D X-rays IV Aircraft navigation

Choose the correct answer from the option given below:
question:
Match List I and List II

  List I   List II
A Microwaves I Physiotherapy
B UV rays II Treatment of cancer
C Infra-red rays III Lasik eye surgery
D X-rays IV Aircraft navigation

Choose the correct answer from the option given below:
solution:
A. Microwave → IV

Microwaves have short wavelength hence low energy dispersion during long distance communication. Thus, they are used in radar system.
B. UV rays → III

LASIK uses an excimer laser (an ultraviolet laser) to remove a thin layer of corneal tissue.

C. Infra-red →I

Infrared radiation in the device called infrared lamp is used in physiotherapy. This light is useful in reducing pain, decreasing inflammation, and speeding up wound care and tissue repair.


D. X-ray → II

Radiation therapy uses high-energy particles or waves, such as x-rays to destroy or damage cancer cells.

=12401.24×106=10-3nm

Where, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>c</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> is the speed of light the corresponding electric filed is :
question: The magnetic field of a plane electromagnetic wave is  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>B</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">→</mo></mover><mo mathvariant="italic">=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo mathvariant="italic">-</mo><mn>8</mn></mrow></msup><mi>sin</mi><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mn>200</mn><mi>π</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>y</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">+</mo><mi>c</mi><mi>t</mi><mo>)</mo></mrow></mfenced><mover><mi>i</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">^</mo></mover><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> . Where, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>c</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> is the speed of light the corresponding electric filed is :
solution:
E0=cB0

∴E→×B→‖C→⇒k^×i^=j^

E→=3×108×3×10-8sin[200πy+ct(-k^)  =9sin[200πy+ct](-k^)

subject: Physics
q: The electric field in an electromagnetic wave is given by $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}=\hat{i} 40 \cos \omega(\mathrm{t}-z / \mathrm{c}) \mathrm{NC}^{-1}$. The magnetic field induction of this wave is (in SI unit) :
question: The electric field in an electromagnetic wave is given by $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}=\hat{i} 40 \cos \omega(\mathrm{t}-z / \mathrm{c}) \mathrm{NC}^{-1}$. The magnetic field induction of this wave is (in SI unit) :
solution:
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}} 40 \cos \omega\left(\mathrm{t}-\frac{\mathrm{z}}{\mathrm{c}}\right)$
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}$ is along $+\mathrm{x}$ direction
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}$ is along $+\mathrm{z}$ direction
So direction of $\vec{B}$ will be along +y and magnitude of $B$ will be $\frac{E}{c}$
So answer is $\frac{40}{c} \cos \omega\left(t-\frac{z}{c}\right) \hat{j}$

subject: Physics
q: A plane electromagnetic wave of frequency <math><mn>50 </mn><mi></mi><mi>M</mi><mi>H</mi><mi>z</mi></math> travels in free space along the positive <math><mi>x</mi></math> - direction. At a particular point in space and time, <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>E</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mn>6.3</mn><mi> </mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mi> </mi><mrow><mrow><mi>V</mi></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><mi>m</mi><mo>,</mo></mrow></mrow></math> The corresponding magnetic field <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>B</mi><mo>,</mo></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></math> at that point will be:
question: A plane electromagnetic wave of frequency <math><mn>50 </mn><mi></mi><mi>M</mi><mi>H</mi><mi>z</mi></math> travels in free space along the positive <math><mi>x</mi></math> - direction. At a particular point in space and time, <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>E</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mn>6.3</mn><mi> </mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mi> </mi><mrow><mrow><mi>V</mi></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><mi>m</mi><mo>,</mo></mrow></mrow></math> The corresponding magnetic field <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>B</mi><mo>,</mo></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></math> at that point will be:
solution:
As we know EB=C

E=6.3V/m

B=EC=6.33×108T=2.1×10-8T

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements : one is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).
Assertion (A) : Electromagnetic waves carry energy but not momentum.
Reason (\(\mathbf{R}\)): Mass of a photon is zero.
In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements : one is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).
Assertion (A) : Electromagnetic waves carry energy but not momentum.
Reason (\(\mathbf{R}\)): Mass of a photon is zero.
In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
options: Both (A) and (R) are true and (R) is the correct explanation of \((\mathbf{A})\)
Both \((\mathbf{A})\) and \((\mathbf{R})\) are true but \((\mathbf{R})\) is not the correct explanation of \((\mathbf{A})\)
solution: EM wave carry both energy and momentum. Rest mass of photon is zero.

subject: Physics
q:
A $27 \mathrm{~mW}$ laser beam has a cross-sectional area of $10 \mathrm{~mm}^{2}$. The magnitude of the maximum electric field in this electromagnetic wave is given by:

[Given permittivity of space $\epsilon_{0}=9 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{SI}$ units, Speed of light $\left.\mathrm{c}=3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\right]$
question:
A $27 \mathrm{~mW}$ laser beam has a cross-sectional area of $10 \mathrm{~mm}^{2}$. The magnitude of the maximum electric field in this electromagnetic wave is given by:

[Given permittivity of space $\epsilon_{0}=9 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{SI}$ units, Speed of light $\left.\mathrm{c}=3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\right]$
solution:
EM wave intensity

$\Rightarrow \mathrm{I}=\frac{\text { Power }}{\text { Area }}=\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_{0} \mathrm{E}_{0}^{2} \mathrm{c}$

[where $\mathrm{E}_{0}=$ maximum electric field] $]$

$\Rightarrow \frac{27 \times 10^{-3}}{10 \times 10^{-6}}=\frac{1}{2} \times 9 \times 10^{-12} \times \mathrm{E}_{0}^{2} \times 3 \times 10^{8}$

$\Rightarrow \mathrm{E}_{0}=\sqrt{2} \times 10^{3} \mathrm{kV} / \mathrm{m}=1.4 \mathrm{kV} / \mathrm{m}$

question:

$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|}
\hline & \text { List-I } & & \text { List-II } \\
\hline & \text { EM-Wave } & & \text { Wavelength Range } \\
\hline \text { (A) } & \text { Infra-red } & \text { (I) } &  < 10^{-3} \mathrm{~nm} \\
\hline \text { (B) } & \text { Ultraviolet } & \text { (II) } & 400 \mathrm{~nm} \text { to } 1 \mathrm{~nm} \\
\hline \text { (C) } & \text { X-rays } S & \text { (III) } & 1 \mathrm{~mm} \text { to } 700 \mathrm{~nm} \\
\hline \text { (D) } & \text { Gamma rays } & \text { (IV) } & 1 \mathrm{~nm} \text { to } 10^{-3} \mathrm{~nm} \\
\hline
\end{array}$
solution: Infrared is the least energetic thus having biggest wavelength $(\lambda) \&$ gamma rays are most energetic thus having smallest wavelength $(\lambda)$.

subject: Physics
q:
The electric field in an electromagnetic wave is given by
E=50 N C-1sinω(t-xc)
The energy contained in a cylinder of volume V is 5.5×10-12 J. The value of V is _______ cm3.
(given ϵ0=8.8×10-12C2 N-1 m-2)
question:
The electric field in an electromagnetic wave is given by
E=50 N C-1sinω(t-xc)
The energy contained in a cylinder of volume V is 5.5×10-12 J. The value of V is _______ cm3.
(given ϵ0=8.8×10-12C2 N-1 m-2)
solution:
Energy density =12ε0E2
Energy =12ε0E2× volume
5.5×10-12=12×8.8×10-12×502×V
V=500 cm3

subject: Physics
q: If Electric field intensity of a uniform plane electro magnetic wave is given as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>301</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>k</mi><mi>z</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>ω</mi><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced><msub><mover><mi>a</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mi>x</mi></msub><mo>+</mo><mn>452</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>k</mi><mi>z</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>ω</mi><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced><msub><mover><mi>a</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mi>y</mi></msub><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> Then, magnetic intensity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>H</mi></math> of this wave in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> will be [Given : Speed of light in vacuum <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>c</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>8</mn></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, Permeability of vacuum <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>μ</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>π</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>7</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>]
question: If Electric field intensity of a uniform plane electro magnetic wave is given as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>301</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>k</mi><mi>z</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>ω</mi><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced><msub><mover><mi>a</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mi>x</mi></msub><mo>+</mo><mn>452</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>k</mi><mi>z</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>ω</mi><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced><msub><mover><mi>a</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mi>y</mi></msub><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> Then, magnetic intensity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>H</mi></math> of this wave in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> will be [Given : Speed of light in vacuum <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>c</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>8</mn></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, Permeability of vacuum <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>μ</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>π</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>7</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>]
solution:
The relation between magnetic field and electric field is given by,

c=E0B0⇒B0=E0c

And magnetic intensity is given by,

H0=B0μ0⇒H0=E0cμ0

Also, E→ and B→ are perpendicular to each other.

Therefore,

Hy0=301.63×108×4π×10-7=0.8 A m-1Hx0=452.43×108×4π×10-7=1.2 A m-1

Hence, C is the answer.

subject: Physics
q: The electromagnetic waves travel in a medium at a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>8</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The relative permeability of the medium is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn></math>. The relative permittivity of the medium will be
question: The electromagnetic waves travel in a medium at a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>8</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The relative permeability of the medium is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn></math>. The relative permittivity of the medium will be
solution:
Here, v=2×108 m s−1 and μr=1

The speed of electromagnetic waves in a medium is given by v=1με  ...i

where μ and ε are absolute permeability and absolute permittivity of the medium.
Now, μ=μ0μr and ε=ε0εr
Then, we have, 

v=1μ0μr×ε0εr=1μ0ε0×1μrεr=cμrεr

orεr=c2v2μr=3×10822×1082=94=2.25

subject: Physics
q: An electromagnetic wave of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mi>GHz</mi></math>, is travelling in a medium whose relative electric permittivity and relative magnetic permeability both are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo></math> Its velocity in this medium is _______<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>7</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>.
question: An electromagnetic wave of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mi>GHz</mi></math>, is travelling in a medium whose relative electric permittivity and relative magnetic permeability both are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo></math> Its velocity in this medium is _______<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>7</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>.
solution:
Given : Frequency of wave f=5GHz

=5×109 Hz

Relative permittivity, ϵr=2 and Relative permeability, μr=2 Since speed of light in a medium is given by,

v=1μ∈=1μrμ0·ϵr∈0

v=1μrϵf1μ0ϵ0=Cμrϵr

Where C is speed of light is vacuum

∴ v=3×1084=30×1072 m s-1

=15×107 m s-1

∴ Ans. is 15
